replies.author: @RWBHere
replies.text: Google also has bots which give automatic downvotes. These are up to 3 - 5% of the number of Likes. They are used to swing listings positions for certain videos, in order to maximise profit for Google. Greedy,, of course. But the thing to remember is that only some of the dislikes are from real people.

author: @SoundsLikeOdie
text: I had access to the dummy lenses from glasses. They are a similar material. I placed some in a medicine bottle and filled it with acetone. They melt away slowly and once the acetone is gone solidify (with 2 or 3 bubbles in my case). I was thinking of using the syrup to make prisms  as you mentioned.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's the silica abrasive in there mate - if you want top drawer go for the cerumium oxide 0 but tooth paste is well worth a go as it really can't do any harm - fancy trying it and letting me know how it goes?

replies.author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I've used brasso then toothpaste for removing scratches from car headlights, which are a pretty similar material, came out as good as new.  Used the same technology - orbital sander with a bit of cloth on it.

If you're looking for a really optical quality surface, of course, orbital sander and elbow grease isn't going to cut it, you'll need to lap very carefully using the three plate method.

author: @olivierroy1301
text: I would try acetone vapors to have an even finish. I know it works with 3d printings.

replies.author: @SoundsLikeOdie
replies.text: Yes I was thinking that too. Or make a thin dam on the textured side and store it in a dust free environment so it can evaporate away.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's called fume polishing mate - it is an ok technique but for optical quality a little spotty

replies.author: @kde5fan737
replies.text:
Have you ever tried MEK?  I forget which is more volatile and evaporates faster.  I'm thinking both should be usable for this though I'm not 100% sure. 

Also, look for headlight refinishing kits, they will have everything you need to do this in one pack.  It might not get it to optical grade but it should be plenty clear for general use.  

I did find the cerium oxide polishing powder on ebay for about $2 per ounce, so that is a really good price, just make sure you get polishing grade and not the general powder b/c the polishing grade usually has a maximum particle size where none graded can have anything.

author: @kde5fan737
text: I think acetone vapor will give the best finish.  You don't put it on the acrylic you basically blow the vapor from the bottle over the surface and it melts all the little peaks and they fill in the valleys and it ends up very flat and smooth and incredibly clear.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cerium oxide polishing really is the best mate - fume polishing is ok for sure - but not good enough for optical quality

replies.author: @kde5fan737
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Yeah, I agree if they are going to use it for optical but for general use (which is what I was thinking most would be doing), I thought acetone would be the quickest, easiest, most readily available and probably least expensive method if you just want a piece of clear acrylic for something like a picture frame or whatever.  
On a side note, the next time you get a few of these pieces, stack them up and spin them around the center of the glass (keeping one piece still )and you will see some very interesting repeating patterns.  I have 3 different pieces from 3 different monitors (so all slightly diff sizes) and it makes some really neat patterns.  
Do you have any suggestions on how to get the acrylic to bond to glass?  I was thinking of trying to make some bullet resistant glass for fun and want to try with some of these pieces.  
Another source of large pieces of acrylic (or polycarbonate, IDK which it is), is from the old projection TV's that were made from mid 90's - ~2007.  They have a massive fresnel lens and a piece of 3/16 - 1/4" acrylic as the front surface, as well as some interesting projector bulbs and other electronic circuits (many have completely separate audio amp board that are 10-50 watts / channel that can be used very easily)..

replies.author: @sofa-lofa4241
replies.text: @kde5fan737 Projector T. V.'s are a great source of parts, I butchered one about 10 years ago for the acrylic screen.... Then found out the hard way that the RGB lenses are oil filled, lol
